lyrics.jp: 大丈夫、痛みにとっても弱いから
lyrics.romaji: Daijoubu, itami ni tottemo yowai kara
lyrics.en: It's okay, because I'm very weak when it comes to pain

breakdown.meaning: very, extremely (casual form of とても)

learn.grammar: 〜から at the end of a clause means 'because' or 'since'. It gives the reason for what came before. Pattern: [reason]から、[result].
learn.literal: Okay — toward pain very weak, because

example.jp: 疲れているから、早く寝る。
example.romaji: Tsukarete iru kara, hayaku neru.
example.en: Because I'm tired, I'll go to bed early.

lyrics.jp: 大丈夫、憧れは時に憧れのまんま
lyrics.romaji: Daijoubu, akogare wa toki ni akogare no manma
lyrics.en: It's okay, sometimes our yearnings stay exactly as yearnings

breakdown.meaning: topic particle — marks the subject of discussion
as-is, just as it is (casual form of まま)

learn.grammar: 〜のまま (or casual まんま) means 'remaining as it is'. Pattern: [noun/state]のまま = still in that state, unchanged. The word repeats (憧れ…憧れ) for poetic emphasis.
learn.literal: Okay — yearning sometimes as yearning's as-is

example.jp: 夢は夢のままでいい。
example.romaji: Yume wa yume no mama de ii.
example.en: It's okay for a dream to stay just a dream.

lyrics.jp: 愛、ジョーク、それとたまにキツめのネガティブ
lyrics.romaji: Ai, jooku, sore to tama ni kitsume no negatibu
lyrics.en: Love, jokes, and sometimes a touch of harsh negativity

learn.grammar: 〜め is a suffix added to adjective stems meaning 'somewhat [adj]' or 'on the [adj] side'. キツい (harsh) → キツめ (a bit harsh). It softens or hedges the quality.
learn.literal: Love, joke, and occasionally somewhat-harsh negativity

example.jp: 今日は早めに起きた。
example.romaji: Kyou wa hayame ni okita.
example.en: Today I woke up a bit on the early side.

lyrics.jp: それでいつも元通りさ
lyrics.romaji: Sore de itsumo moto doori sa
lyrics.en: And yet we always go back to how we were

breakdown.meaning: and so, with that, and yet
back to the original state, back to normal

learn.grammar: さ as a sentence-ending particle adds a casual, slightly assertive tone — like 'you know' or 'that's just how it is'. It's softer and more resigned than よ.
learn.literal: With that, always back-to-original — you know

example.jp: 喧嘩しても、いつも元通りさ。
example.romaji: Kenka shite mo, itsumo moto doori sa.
example.en: Even after a fight, we always go back to normal, you know.

lyrics.jp: 君と僕との間柄なら　そりゃ出くわす問題もさまざま
lyrics.romaji: Kimi to boku to no aidagara nara sorya dekuwasu mondai mo samazama
lyrics.en: Given the relationship between you and me, of course we'll run into all kinds of problems

breakdown.meaning: the nature of a relationship, how two people relate
of course, naturally (casual form of それは)
to run into, to encounter unexpectedly

learn.grammar: 〜なら is a conditional meaning 'if it's [X], given that [X]'. It accepts a situation as a premise. Different from 〜たら (event sequence) — なら frames context/premise.
learn.literal: You and me's relationship if — of-course encounter problems also various

example.jp: 友達なら、助け合うのは当然だ。
example.romaji: Tomodachi nara, tasuke au no wa touzen da.
example.en: If you're friends, it's natural to help each other.

lyrics.jp: だから隣にいりゃ旅の道すがら　どんな闇からも命からがら
lyrics.romaji: Dakara tonari ni irya tabi no michisugara donna yami kara mo inochi karagara
lyrics.en: So as long as you're beside me on life's journey, we'll barely escape any darkness

breakdown.meaning: along the way, while on the road
any kind of, whatever kind of
barely escaping with one's life, by the skin of one's teeth

learn.grammar: 〜いれば contracts to 〜いりゃ in casual/song speech: 'if [someone] is there'. Contractions like this are common in lyrics. いれば → いりゃ, すれば → すりゃ, etc.
learn.literal: That's why, beside if-there, journey's along-the-way, any darkness even from, barely-with-life

example.jp: 隣にいてくれるから、怖くない。
example.romaji: Tonari ni ite kureru kara, kowakunai.
example.en: Because you're beside me, I'm not scared.

lyrics.jp: 助けるよ　助けてよ　My Buddy Darling
lyrics.romaji: Tasukeru yo tasukete yo My Buddy Darling
lyrics.en: I'll save you, save me, My Buddy Darling

breakdown.meaning: to help, to save, to rescue [verb]

learn.grammar: The て-form of a verb used alone as a sentence is a request or plea: 助けて = 'please help me!'. The same verb appears twice — first asserting (I will save you) then pleading (save me). This mirrors the mutual nature of the relationship.
learn.literal: Will-save [asserting]! Save-me [pleading]! My Buddy Darling

example.jp: 助けてください！道に迷いました。
example.romaji: Tasukete kudasai! Michi ni mayoimashita.
example.en: Please help me! I got lost.

lyrics.jp: SOSが不器用でも気にしないからもう
lyrics.romaji: SOS ga bukiyou demo ki ni shinai kara mou
lyrics.en: Even if your SOS is clumsy, I don't mind anymore

breakdown.meaning: SOS — emergency signal, cry for help
to not mind, to not care, to not worry about
already, anymore, now (implies a change or decision)

learn.grammar: 〜でも means 'even if' or 'even though' — it concedes a point while dismissing its importance. Pattern: [noun/adj]でも、[main claim]. Note: でも at the start of a sentence means 'but/however' — a different usage.
learn.literal: SOS subject clumsy even-if, don't-mind because, now

example.jp: 下手でも、一生懸命やっていれば気にしない。
example.romaji: Heta demo, isshoukenmei yatte ireba ki ni shinai.
example.en: Even if you're not good at it, I don't mind as long as you're trying hard.

lyrics.jp: 素っ気ないくらいで僕らはちょうどいいんじゃない？
lyrics.romaji: Sokkenai kurai de bokura wa choudo iin janai?
lyrics.en: Isn't it just right for us to be a little cold and distant?

breakdown.meaning: at the level of, to that extent (degree marker)
isn't it? isn't that the case? (casual rhetorical question)

learn.grammar: 〜くらいで means 'at the level of [X]'. Here: 'being THIS cold is just the right amount for us'. 〜んじゃない？ is a very common spoken rhetorical question tag — softer than でしょう？
learn.literal: Cold-and-indifferent level-of, we subject just-right isn't-it?

example.jp: これくらいの距離感がちょうどいい。
example.romaji: Kore kurai no kyori-kan ga choudo ii.
example.en: This level of distance between us is just right.

lyrics.jp: きっと涙も言葉もおまけでもいいんじゃない？
lyrics.romaji: Kitto namida mo kotoba mo omake demo iin janai?
lyrics.en: Surely it's fine even if tears and words are just extras, right?

breakdown.meaning: bonus, extra, freebie, something thrown in
it's okay even if [X] / [X] is fine too

learn.grammar: 〜でもいい means 'it's fine even if it's [X]'. AもBもでもいい = 'it's okay if it's A, or B, or whatever'. The も〜も structure lists multiple items in a 'both A and B' pattern.
learn.literal: Surely tears also words also bonus even-if okay isn't-it?

example.jp: うまく言えなくてもいいんじゃない？
example.romaji: Umaku ienakute mo iin janai?
example.en: Isn't it okay even if you can't say it well?

lyrics.jp: 大丈夫、傷跡は時に勲章になって
lyrics.romaji: Daijoubu, kizuato wa toki ni kunshou ni natte
lyrics.en: It's okay, sometimes scars become medals of honor

breakdown.meaning: becoming (〜に + なる in て-form)

learn.grammar: 〜になる means 'to become [X]'. The て-form なって continues the sentence or implies an ongoing state. 勲章になって = 'turning into a medal, becoming a medal'.
learn.literal: Okay — scars topic sometimes medal becoming

example.jp: 失敗は時に成功の勲章になる。
example.romaji: Shippai wa toki ni seikou no kunshou ni naru.
example.en: Failures sometimes become medals of success.

lyrics.jp: 大丈夫、迷子は時に旅人になって
lyrics.romaji: Daijoubu, maigo wa toki ni tabibito ni natte
lyrics.en: It's okay, sometimes the lost become travelers

breakdown.meaning: lost child, someone who is lost and wandering

learn.grammar: This line mirrors the previous one (傷跡→勲章, 迷子→旅人) using the same 〜は時に〜になって pattern. Parallelism (対句・たいく) is a core Japanese poetic technique — repeating structure with different words to build layered meaning.
learn.literal: Okay — lost-one topic sometimes traveler becoming

example.jp: 迷子になったことで、素敵な場所を見つけた。
example.romaji: Maigo ni natta koto de, suteki na basho wo mitsuketa.
example.en: By getting lost, I found a wonderful place.

lyrics.jp: 夢、希望、それと時に怠惰と言い訳
lyrics.romaji: Yume, kibou, sore to toki ni taida to iiwake
lyrics.en: Dreams, hope, and sometimes laziness and excuses too

learn.grammar: AとB lists nouns exhaustively — 'A and B (and that's all)'. This line follows the verse 1 pattern (愛・ジョーク・ネガティブ) but swaps in darker words. The song admits laziness and excuses are part of being human, not just love.
learn.literal: Dreams, hope, and also sometimes laziness and excuses

example.jp: 言い訳ばかりしても何も変わらない。
example.romaji: Iiwake bakari shite mo nani mo kawaranai.
example.en: Nothing changes even if you keep making excuses.

lyrics.jp: 全部ひっくるめて僕らさ
lyrics.romaji: Zenbu hikkuruMete bokura sa
lyrics.en: All of it together — that's us

breakdown.meaning: lumping everything together, all included (て-form of ひっくるめる)

learn.grammar: ひっくるめる is a verb meaning 'to bundle everything together'. て-form here acts as 'with everything bundled up'. 全部 + ひっくるめて = 'every single bit of it, all lumped together'. The を (object particle) before 全部 is dropped — common in casual speech.
learn.literal: Everything all-lumped-together, we — you know

example.jp: いいところも悪いところも全部ひっくるめて、あなたが好きだ。
example.romaji: Ii tokoro mo warui tokoro mo zenbu hikkuruMete, anata ga suki da.
example.en: I love you — good parts, bad parts, and everything in between.

lyrics.jp: 君と僕との間柄なら　そりゃ見せたくない部分もさまざま
lyrics.romaji: Kimi to boku to no aidagara nara sorya misetakunai bubun mo samazama
lyrics.en: Given the relationship between you and me, there are all kinds of parts we don't want to show each other

breakdown.meaning: relationship, the nature of how two people relate
don't want to show (見せる + たい + ない)

learn.grammar: 〜たくない is the negative of 〜たい ('want to'). 見せたい = want to show → 見せたくない = don't want to show. Pattern: [verb stem] + たい (want to) / たくない (don't want to).
learn.literal: You and me's relationship if — of-course don't-want-to-show aspects also various

example.jp: 彼に見せたくない部分がある。
example.romaji: Kare ni misetakunai bubun ga aru.
example.en: There are aspects of myself I don't want to show him.

lyrics.jp: だから隣にいりゃ過去の道すがら　どんな傷も笑い話
lyrics.romaji: Dakara tonari ni irya kako no michisugara donna kizu mo waraibanashi
lyrics.en: So as long as you're beside me through our past, any wound becomes a funny story

breakdown.meaning: along the way, while on the path
funny story, something you can laugh about later

learn.grammar: Japanese often omits the verb at the end for poetic effect: どんな傷も笑い話 stops at 'wound → funny story' — the verb になる ('becomes') is implied. This ellipsis (省略・しょうりゃく) is very common in Japanese poetry and song.
learn.literal: That's why beside if-there, past's along-the-way, any wound even, funny-story [becomes]

example.jp: 今では笑い話になったけど、当時はつらかった。
example.romaji: Ima de wa waraibanashi ni natta kedo, touji wa tsurakatta.
example.en: It's become a funny story now, but it was painful at the time.

lyrics.jp: 助けるよ　助けてよ　My Buddy Darling
lyrics.romaji: Tasukeru yo tasukete yo My Buddy Darling
lyrics.en: I'll save you, save me, My Buddy Darling

breakdown.meaning: to help, to save, to rescue

learn.grammar: Chorus repeat — same words, new emotional context from verse 2. The song has now acknowledged not just pain but laziness and the things we hide. The mutual plea feels more vulnerable the second time.
learn.literal: Will-save [asserting]! Save-me [pleading]! My Buddy Darling

example.jp: 困ったときは助け合うのが友達だ。
example.romaji: Komatta toki wa tasuke au no ga tomodachi da.
example.en: Friends are people who help each other in times of trouble.

lyrics.jp: 涙が不格好でも気にしないからもう
lyrics.romaji: Namida ga bukakkou demo ki ni shinai kara mou
lyrics.en: Even if your tears look ugly, I don't mind anymore

breakdown.meaning: already, anymore — implies a shift or decision

learn.grammar: This chorus line swaps 'SOS' → '涙' (tears) compared to the first chorus. A key J-pop technique: second chorus changes one word per line to deepen emotion while keeping the melody identical. 不格好 pairs the negative prefix 不 + 格好 (appearance/shape).
learn.literal: Tears subject ungainly even-if, don't-mind because, now

example.jp: 泣いてもいいよ。不格好でも気にしないから。
example.romaji: Naite mo ii yo. Bukakkou demo ki ni shinai kara.
example.en: It's okay to cry. I don't mind even if it's not pretty.

lyrics.jp: 素直じゃないくらいで僕らはちょうどいいんじゃない？
lyrics.romaji: Sunao janai kurai de bokura wa choudo iin janai?
lyrics.en: Isn't it just right for us not to be totally honest with each other?

breakdown.meaning: at that level, to that extent

learn.grammar: 素直 (すなお) is an important N4 adjective — it describes being honest, open, easy to deal with. 素直じゃない = not being totally open. This swaps 素っ気ない (cold) from the first chorus for 素直じゃない (not fully honest) — a more emotionally honest reframe.
learn.literal: Honest-not level-of, we just-right isn't-it?

example.jp: 素直に「ありがとう」と言えばいい。
example.romaji: Sunao ni 'arigatou' to ieba ii.
example.en: You should just honestly say 'thank you'.

lyrics.jp: きっと傷も笑顔もタトゥーでもいいんじゃない？
lyrics.romaji: Kitto kizu mo egao mo tattoo demo iin janai?
lyrics.en: Surely it's fine if both our wounds and our smiles are like tattoos, right?

breakdown.meaning: tattoo — the central metaphor of the song

learn.grammar: The title word 'tattoo' appears! It represents what's permanent and indelible. Both wounds (傷) and smiles (笑顔) become tattoos — marks that last. 笑顔 is a beautiful compound: 笑 (laugh/smile) + 顔 (face) = a smiling face.
learn.literal: Surely wounds also smiles also tattoo even-if okay isn't-it?

example.jp: 彼との思い出は、心に刻まれたタトゥーのようだ。
example.romaji: Kare to no omoide wa, kokoro ni kizamareta tattoo no you da.
example.en: The memories with him are like a tattoo carved into my heart.

lyrics.jp: 刻まれた日々が証明するよ
lyrics.romaji: Kizamareta hibi ga shoumei suru yo
lyrics.en: The days carved into us will prove it

breakdown.meaning: carved, engraved, etched (past passive of 刻む)

learn.grammar: 〜られた is the passive past tense: 刻む (to carve) → 刻まれた (was carved / has been carved). Passive voice in Japanese marks that the subject received the action. 刻まれた日々 = 'the days that have been carved into us'. This echoes the tattoo metaphor — the past is literally carved in.
learn.literal: Carved days subject will-prove (emphasis)

example.jp: 二人で過ごした時間が、本物だと証明してくれる。
example.romaji: Futari de sugoshita jikan ga, honmono da to shoumei shite kureru.
example.en: The time we spent together proves that it was real.

lyrics.jp: 君といた時間は嘘じゃない
lyrics.romaji: Kimi to ita jikan wa uso ja nai
lyrics.en: The time I spent with you is not a lie

breakdown.meaning: was there, existed (past tense of いる)

learn.grammar: 〜じゃない is the casual negative of だ/です. 嘘だ = 'it's a lie' → 嘘じゃない = 'it's not a lie'. This direct, simple sentence is emotionally the most powerful in the song — just 8 syllables, no complex grammar. Simplicity is a feature.
learn.literal: You with was time topic lie is-not

example.jp: あの頃の気持ちは嘘じゃなかった。
example.romaji: Ano koro no kimochi wa uso ja nakatta.
example.en: The feelings I had back then were not a lie.

lyrics.jp: たとえ形が変わったとしても
lyrics.romaji: Tatoe katachi ga kawatta to shite mo
lyrics.en: Even if the shape of things changes

breakdown.meaning: even if, supposing that (sets up a hypothetical)
even if it is the case that (concessive)

learn.grammar: たとえ〜としても is a set grammar pattern: 'even if / even supposing that'. Always paired — たとえ at the start sets the hypothetical, としても (or ても) closes it. Very common in emotional/philosophical contexts.
learn.literal: Even-if shape subject changed, even-supposing-that

example.jp: たとえ遠く離れても、友達だ。
example.romaji: Tatoe tooku hanarete mo, tomodachi da.
example.en: Even if we're far apart, we're still friends.

lyrics.jp: この胸に残る　消えないタトゥー
lyrics.romaji: Kono mune ni nokoru kienai tattoo
lyrics.en: The tattoo that won't fade, remaining in this chest

learn.grammar: 消える (to disappear/fade) → 消えない (won't disappear). Adding ない to the plain form makes it negative non-past. 消えないタトゥー = 'a tattoo that won't fade'. The noun-modifier structure: [verb clause]タトゥー = 'a tattoo that [verbs]'. This is the climax of the song.
learn.literal: This chest in remains, won't-fade tattoo

example.jp: あの思い出は消えないタトゥーのように胸に刻まれている。
example.romaji: Ano omoide wa kienai tattoo no you ni mune ni kizamarete iru.
example.en: That memory is carved into my chest like an unfading tattoo.

lyrics.jp: 助けるよ　助けてよ　My Buddy Darling
lyrics.romaji: Tasukeru yo tasukete yo My Buddy Darling
lyrics.en: I'll save you, save me, My Buddy Darling

breakdown.meaning: to help, to save, to rescue

learn.grammar: The final chorus — the same words as the first, but after the bridge's revelation (the days carved in us are proof; the time was not a lie). In Japanese music, repetition builds meaning through accumulated context. 助ける now feels like a lifelong vow.
learn.literal: Will-save! Save-me! My Buddy Darling

example.jp: 助け合いながら、一緒に生きていこう。
example.romaji: Tasuke ai nagara, issho ni ikite ikou.
example.en: Let's live together while helping each other.

lyrics.jp: SOSが不器用でも気にしないからもう
lyrics.romaji: SOS ga bukiyou demo ki ni shinai kara mou
lyrics.en: Even if your SOS is clumsy, I don't mind anymore

learn.grammar: もう here signals a decisive shift: 'I won't mind anymore / I've decided'. This もう carries emotional finality, stronger than just 'already'. Compare: もう食べた = 'I already ate' vs もう気にしない = 'I'm done worrying — it's settled'.
learn.literal: SOS clumsy even-if, don't-mind because, now/anymore

example.jp: もう気にしないことにした。
example.romaji: Mou ki ni shinai koto ni shita.
example.en: I decided I'm not going to worry about it anymore.

lyrics.jp: 素っ気ないくらいで僕らはちょうどいいんじゃない？
lyrics.romaji: Sokkenai kurai de bokura wa choudo iin janai?
lyrics.en: Isn't it just right for us to be a little cold and distant?

breakdown.meaning: at that level, to that extent

learn.grammar: Final appearance of this line. After the whole song, 素っ気ない no longer reads as emotional coldness — it reads as acceptance of imperfection. This is how J-pop lyrics work: identical words carry different emotional weight depending on where in the song you hear them.
learn.literal: Cold-indifferent level-of, we just-right isn't-it?

example.jp: お互いのことを大切に思いながらも、ちょうどいい距離感でいよう。
example.romaji: Otagai no koto wo taisetsu ni ominagara mo, choudo ii kyorikan de iyou.
example.en: While caring for each other, let's maintain just the right distance.

lyrics.jp: きっと涙も言葉もおまけでもいいんじゃない？
lyrics.romaji: Kitto namida mo kotoba mo omake demo iin janai?
lyrics.en: Surely it's fine even if tears and words are just extras, right?

breakdown.meaning: bonus, extra — not the main thing, but thrown in

learn.grammar: Final appearance. おまけ (extra/bonus) is a powerful word choice — it says that tears and words are nice to have, but not required. The love (the tattoo) exists beyond expression. This creates the central philosophical statement: some things are carved into you without needing words.
learn.literal: Surely tears also words also bonus even-if okay isn't-it?

example.jp: 言葉がなくても、気持ちは伝わる。
example.romaji: Kotoba ga nakute mo, kimochi wa tsutawaru.
example.en: Even without words, feelings get through.

lyrics.jp: 助けるよ　助けてよ　My Buddy Darling
lyrics.romaji: Tasukeru yo tasukete yo My Buddy Darling
lyrics.en: I'll save you, save me, My Buddy Darling

learn.grammar: The song ends on this mutual pledge — the same as the beginning. Japanese value 循環 (junkan — cyclical structure) in storytelling. Ending where you began signals not stagnation but a completed journey: the same words, a transformed relationship.
learn.literal: Will-save! Save-me! My Buddy Darling

example.jp: ずっと側にいて、助け合っていこう。
example.romaji: Zutto soba ni ite, tasuke atte ikou.
example.en: Let's always be by each other's side and help one another.

lyrics.jp: いつまでもどこまでも　My Buddy Darling
lyrics.romaji: Itsumade mo doko made mo My Buddy Darling
lyrics.en: Forever and wherever — My Buddy Darling

breakdown.meaning: wherever, to any extent — literally 'even to wherever'

learn.grammar: 〜までも = まで ('until/to') + も ('even'). いつまでも = 'even until any time = forever'. どこまでも = 'even to anywhere = wherever'. These two together express total commitment across both time and space — the ultimate unconditional promise to close the song.
learn.literal: Until-whenever-even, to-wherever-even, My Buddy Darling

example.jp: いつまでも、どこにいても、あなたのことを思っている。
example.romaji: Itsumade mo, doko ni ite mo, anata no koto wo omotte iru.
example.en: No matter how long or where you are, I'll always be thinking of you.
